s_text:
**Verifiers and Reward Models for Agent Learning.**
Verifiers that assign rewards to agent behavior have been central to advances in AI, from game-playing systems guided by outcome-based rewards to large reasoning models trained with formal verifiers in code and math. In open-ended domains such as web navigation, desktop automation, and robotics, where success criteria are nuanced and multimodal, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have emerged as promising general-purpose verifiers. Prior work has explored MLLMs in evaluative roles through benchmarks such as Agent-RewardBench [1], which evaluates reward modeling across perception, planning, and safety in real-world multimodal agents, and through agentic verification frameworks such as VAGEN [2], which proactively interacts with GUI environments to overcome partial observability. Process-level supervision has also been studied for GUI agents: GUI-Shepherd [3] trains a process reward model to provide dense, step-by-step feedback, while ProgRM [4] introduces progress rewards estimated via longest-common-subsequence-based self-annotation to address sparse feedback in GUI reinforcement learning. These efforts highlight the potential of MLLM-based rewards, yet also reveal challenges in ensuring reliable verification when criteria are open-ended.

**Biases and Limitations of LLM and MLLM Judges.**
A growing body of work has documented systematic biases in LLM-as-a-Judge settings. Position bias, where judgments are influenced by the order in which candidates are presented, has been studied extensively [5]. Self-preference bias, in which models favor their own outputs, has been shown to correlate with self-recognition capabilities [6] and with perplexity-based familiarity [7]. More broadly, human and LLM judges exhibit multiple judgment biases, including leniency and authority effects, that can be exploited to manipulate evaluations [8]. Recent work has sought to disentangle true self-preference from confounds due to evaluator uncertainty on difficult queries [9], and to mitigate self-preference through structured multi-dimensional evaluation [10]. These biases are particularly concerning when MLLM verifiers are used for failure detection, online supervision, and self-improvement, where skewed judgments can propagate into training signals.

**Self-Improvement and Online Supervision with Verifier Feedback.**
Beyond offline evaluation, verifiers are used to steer and improve agents during or after execution. Reflexion [11] introduced a framework in which language agents reflect on task feedback, store verbal summaries in episodic memory, and use them to guide future decisions without fine-tuning. However, the effectiveness of such self-critiquing depends heavily on the quality of the verifier. Empirical studies have found that LLM-based self-verification can suffer from high false-positive rates, diminishing rather than improving plan quality [12]. In online settings, reinforcement learning has been applied to train GUI agents with trajectory-aware rewards [13], and process reward models have been explored to provide denser feedback than sparse outcome rewards [14]. Implicit process reward modeling [15] further enables online updates without expensive step-level annotations. These lines of work underscore that the utility of a verifier is determined not only by aggregate accuracy but also by its calibration and its ability to correctly identify failures.

**Benchmarks for Multimodal Agents and Evaluation.**
The development of MLLM verifiers has been shaped by benchmarks that demand multimodal reasoning in interactive environments. VisualWebArena [16] evaluates agents on visually grounded web tasks requiring both image and text understanding. OSWorld [17] provides a scalable, real-computer environment for open-ended tasks across operating systems, revealing large gaps between agent and human performance. Complementary benchmarks such as Windows Agent Arena [18] and stress-testing frameworks [19] further probe robustness under realistic variability. These environments serve as critical testbeds for assessing whether MLLM verifiers can generalize across diverse tasks and modalities, and whether their judgments align with human intent.
s_references:
[1] Tianyi Men, Zhuoran Jin, Pengfei Cao, Yubo Chen, Kang Liu, and Weiran Wang. "Agent-RewardBench: Towards a Unified Benchmark for Reward Modeling across Perception, Planning, and Safety in Real-World Multimodal Agents." arXiv:2506.21252, 2025.

[2] Chaoqun Cui, Jing Huang, Shijing Wang, Liming Zheng, Qingchao Kong, and Zhiyong Wu. "Agentic Reward Modeling: Verifying GUI Agent via Online Proactive Interaction." arXiv:2602.00575, 2026.

[3] Cong Chen, Kaixiang Ji, Hao Zhong, Muzhi Zhu, Anzhou Li, and others. "GUI-Shepherd: Reliable Process Reward and Verification for Long-Sequence GUI Tasks." arXiv:2509.23738, 2025.

[4] Danyang Zhang, Situo Zhang, Ziyue Yang, Zichen Zhu, Zihan Zhao, and others. "ProgRM: Build Better GUI Agents with Progress Rewards." arXiv:2505.18121, 2025.

[5] Lin Shi, Chiyu Ma, Wenhua Liang, Xingjian Diao, Weicheng Ma, and Soroush Vosoughi. "Judging the Judges: A Systematic Study of Position Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge." arXiv:2406.07791, 2024.

[6] Arjun Panickssery, Samuel R. Bowman, and Shi Feng. "LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations." arXiv:2404.13076, 2024.

[7] Koki Wataoka, Tsubasa Takahashi, and Ryokan Ri. "Self-Preference Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge." arXiv:2410.21819, 2024.

[8] Guiming Hardy Chen, Shunian Chen, Ziche Liu, Feng Jiang, and Benyou Wang. "Humans or LLMs as the Judge? A Study on Judgement Biases." arXiv:2402.10669, 2024.

[9] Dani Roytburg, Matthew Bozoukov, Matthew Nguyen, Mackenzie Puig-Hall, and Narmeen Oozeer. "Are LLM Evaluators Really Narcissists? Sanity Checking Self-Preference Evaluations." arXiv:2601.22548, 2026.

[10] Jinming Yang, Chuxian Qiu, Zhenyu Deng, Xinshan Jiao, and Tao Zhou. "Quantifying and Mitigating Self-Preference Bias of LLM Judges." arXiv:2604.22891, 2026.

[11] Noah Shinn, Federico Cassano, Edward Berman, Ashwin Gopinath, Karthik Narasimhan, and Shunyu Yao. "Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning." arXiv:2303.11366, 2023.

[12] Karthik Valmeekam, Matthew Marquez, and Subbarao Kambhampati. "Can Large Language Models Really Improve by Self-critiquing Their Own Plans?" arXiv:2310.08118, 2023.

[13] Yucheng Shi, Wenhao Yu, Zaitang Li, Yonglin Wang, Hongming Zhang, and others. "MobileGUI-RL: Advancing Mobile GUI Agent through Reinforcement Learning in Online Environment." arXiv:2507.05720, 2025.

[14] Sanjiban Choudhury. "Process Reward Models for LLM Agents: Practical Framework and Directions." arXiv:2502.10325, 2025.

[15] Ganqu Cui, Lifan Yuan, Zefan Wang, Hanbin Wang, Yuchen Zhang, and others. "Process Reinforcement through Implicit Rewards." arXiv:2502.01456, 2025.

[16] Jing Yu Koh, Robert Lo, Lawrence Jang, Vikram Duvvur, Ming Chong Lim, and others. "VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks." arXiv:2401.13649, 2024.

[17] Tianbao Xie, Danyang Zhang, Jixuan Chen, Xiaochuan Li, Siheng Zhao, and others. "OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real Computer Environments." arXiv:2404.07972, 2024.

[18] Rogerio Bonatti, Dan Zhao, Francesco Bonacci, Dillon Dupont, Sara Abdali, and others. "Windows Agent Arena: Evaluating Multi-Modal OS Agents at Scale." arXiv:2409.08264, 2024.

[19] InclusionAI AWorld-RL. "StressWeb: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Web Agent Robustness under Realistic Interaction Variability." arXiv:2604.16385, 2026.
g_text:
\section{Related Work} \label{sec:related}
\looseness=-1
\textbf{MLLMs as Evaluators.}
(M)LLMs have been employed as evaluators of model outputs in various scenarios under various names---(M)LLMs as judges, critics, reward models, value functions.
This work focuses on multimodal and environment-interaction scenarios.
MLLMs have been used to score and filter agent trajectories for subsequent use in finetuning~, and test-time refinements such as to induce prompts, reflections, and tools~.
They have also served as a source of real-time feedback by producing natural language ``critiques''~, scores to rank action proposals in search~, and rewards for training~.

\looseness=-1
\textbf{AI Agents.}
There is growing interest in building AI agents to act in various environments, including the web~, mobile phones~, and computers~.
In this field, the work most closely related to ours is~, which uses a GPT-4V-based evaluator, prompted with benchmark-specific rubrics, to evaluate trajectories for Reflexion~ and behavior cloning.
Following works employ a similar evaluator to guide tree search~, filter trajectories to generate text-based memories or tools~ to boost agent performance in (Visual)WebArena~, and for RL training in simpler environments~.

\looseness=-1
\textbf{Test-time scaling} (TTS) is a major paradigm to improve model performance without increasing parameters~.
Early work~ shows that prompting LLMs to generate ``chains-of-thought'' yields substantial gains in reasoning-oriented tasks.
This idea has been extended to several settings, including multimodal~ and environment-interaction~.
Orthogonal approaches scale test-time compute via sampling and search~, where multiple generations are selected through heuristics~ or verifiers~.
Recent work leverages sampling, RL, and formal verifiers to train (M)LLMs that autonomously generate reasoning traces~.
Extending these methods to open-ended problems requires flexible and multimodal verification, for which MLLMs offer an appealing solution.

Compared to this body of work, we
\textbf{(1)} consolidate disparate applications of MLLM evaluators under a shared framework based on multimodal rewards derived from MLLM verifiers.
\textbf{(2)} Evaluate MLLM verifiers across a broader range of models, multimodal benchmarks, agents, TTS techniques, evaluation templates, and applications.
\textbf{(3)} Dissect MLLM verifier performance over fine-grained metrics, offering guidance on how to measure the quality of their evaluations and artifacts derived from them.
\textbf{(4)} Identify agreement bias, show its resilience to TTS techniques, and the risks it poses for downstream applications.
\textbf{(5)} Discuss several design choices for building MLLM verifiers and introduce SGV, a lightweight method easy to integrate into pipelines involving MLLM verifiers.
\textbf{(6)} Improve MLLM verification with benefits extending to downstream applications such as online supervision and self-improvement, achieving a new state of the art on VisualWebArena.
